title: Economic Bulletin Issue 2, 2026
summary: The ECB's March 19, 2026 Governing Council meeting resulted in keeping the three key ECB interest rates unchanged. Staff projections show headline inflation revised up to 2.6% in 2026 (from December 2025 projections) due to the war in the Middle East driving higher energy prices, while GDP growth was revised down to 0.9% for 2026. February 2026 HICP inflation was 1.9%, up from 1.7% in January.
key_points: ECB Governing Council kept three key interest rates unchanged at March 19, 2026 meeting
Staff headline inflation projections: 2.6% in 2026, 2.0% in 2027, 2.1% in 2028 — revised up from December 2025 projections due to Middle East war
Staff GDP growth projections: 0.9% in 2026, 1.3% in 2027, 1.4% in 2028 — revised down by 0.3pp for 2026 and 0.1pp for 2027 from December 2025
February 2026 HICP inflation was 1.9%, up from 1.7% in January; inflation excluding energy and food was 2.4%
War in Middle East creating upside risks for inflation and downside risks for growth; scenario analysis shows prolonged energy supply disruption would push inflation above baseline and growth below
Q4 2025 euro area economy grew 0.2% quarter-on-quarter, driven by stronger domestic demand and services
why_it_matters: This ECB Economic Bulletin provides the official staff projections and policy rationale following the March 2026 rate decision. The upward revision to inflation forecasts and downward revision to growth forecasts, driven by Middle East geopolitical tensions, signals the ECB is navigating a more challenging outlook with elevated uncertainty around energy prices. The data-dependent approach means the Governing Council will adjust policy based on incoming information rather than pre-committing to a rate path.
macro_context: The war in the Middle East is the primary source of uncertainty in the ECB's updated projections. Higher energy prices from potential supply disruptions are expected to push headline inflation above the 2% target in the near term, while weighing on real incomes, consumer confidence, and investment. The baseline is conditioned on energy commodity futures prices as of March 11, 2026 cut-off date, assuming relatively rapid reduction in energy prices and uncertainty. Alternative scenarios with prolonged energy shock would result in higher inflation and lower growth. Domestic demand, supported by resilient labor market and government spending on defense and infrastructure, is expected to remain the main growth driver over the medium term.
notes: Source is an official ECB Economic Bulletin publication with high domain trust. The March 19, 2026 Governing Council decision to keep rates unchanged aligns with the no-mention of any rate changes. Published March 2026, within 90 days of TODAY (2026-04-02), making this a current source. The staff projections incorporate information up to March 11, 2026 cut-off date. Source body was truncated but provided sufficient detail on inflation, growth, and policy context. Digital euro and payment infrastructure mentioned but not as primary focus. Balance of views clearly presented with explicit discussion of upside inflation risks and downside growth risks from geopolitical developments.
